content:
If your account is IMAP, then this is exactly what's supposed to happen. Basically an IMAP email client like Thunderbird mirrors everything what's happening on the server. If you prefer to remove messages from the server POP may be the better choice for you. https://support.mozilla.org/kb/faq-changing-imap-pop

When the behavior you're seeing now isn't what you want, then I'm wondering why you set up your account as IMAP in the first place.
creator: christ1

content:
Thank you christ1!

I did not know the difference between IMAP and POP, when I set up Thunderbird.

If I choose POP, then messages will automatically sync, however, when I delete messages in Yahoo, the messages will not be deleted in Thunderbird?
creator: tranland

content: With POP there is no sync. POP can only download messages from Inbox. You can tell Thunderbird to either remove messages from the server, or leave them on the server, after downloading. That's all. Any directory structure you may have created on the server would stay there, but becomes inaccessible for the email client. You should really make yourself familiar with the differences between POP and IMAP protocols.

content:
Try doing it over this way:

Copy TB files from the laptop

- launch TB on the laptop

- go to TB menu > Help > Troubleshooting Information

- click the Application Basics > Profile Folder 'Open Folder' button

- the profile folder will open

- navigate three levels up to the AppData\Roaming folder

- close TB

- copy the AppData\Roaming\Thunderbird folder to a flash drive

---

Paste the copied TB files to the PC

- open the AppData\Roaming folder like you did above

- delete the Thunderbird folder, or copy it somewhere if you want to save it

- replace it with the Thunderbird folder from the flash drive

- launch TB

You're on Windows, so when you don't use a 3rd-party anti-virus solution, you're actually using Microsoft Defender. Anti-virus software is often the culprit when Thunderbird hangs. content:
It is always helpful to let us know what addon theme you have installed. I tested by first setting the Windows theme to Light for apps. Selecting system theme in Thunderbird Downloaded a blue theme eg:'Outlook 2013 Blue' I get blue at top as expected but a dark theme rendered in all other areas. I did notice there were several feedback coments already posted on that addon page stating the same problem.

It seems the addons are only responsible for the top header area and in some cases the Status bar as well. But are not including code to cover all other areas. Only addon themes that specifically include complete code to cover all parts of the UI will control and override that dark theme.

If you are using an addon and there is a problem ot something undesirable, then you need to inform the author of the addon what is expected and what is occuring. Only the author has control over their addon and it's solely their responsibility to make it compatible with the versions of Thunderbird.

you can usually find a link, feedback etc on the addon webpage.

There is an alternative, but it means using a 'userChrome.css' file to control sections of the UI.

content:
what are you changing in the config editor? why?

Seriously I have the time displayed, but generally I have he column width set to not show more that the date and the ellipse that indicates there is more to show. But I mostly do not care about space. By having my email view in the bottom half of the screen (wide view), I have amply horizontal space across my monitor for folder and email lists. Sadly most folk want to emulate that three columns look that Outlook has and then undertake a long standing battle to get everything in a restricted space and have wrapped headings and multi line displays that turns the list into something that is no longer really a list.

I the interests of brevity, I will assume you are messing with; 

mail.ui.display.dateformat.today 	 mail.ui.display.dateformat.thisweek 	 mail.ui.display.dateformat.default

These preferences manage the display of messages for today, this week and everything else respectively. Teey can be set to 

 Value 	Meaning 	Example date and time 0 	No date 	10:23 AM 1 	Your system's long* date format 	Friday, December 31 2003 10:23 AM 2 	Your system's short* date format 	12/31/1999 10:23 AM 3** 	Year and month, separated by a slash 	1999/12 10:23 AM 4** 	Abbreviated day name 	Fri 10:23 AM  * Some operating systems have only one date format. On these systems, the values 1 and 2    produce the same result. ** Values 3 and 4 were not supported in Thunderbird version 53 (due to switching to    ICU/CLDR) but got implemented again in version 54. They stopped working again in    Thunderbird 83. Value 4 (day name) was restored in version 91.

None of that really covers what you are talking about, but it is the only related preference I can think of that has 0-4 as options.

I think what you are seeking is to change the display format of dates in the mail list. The intl.date_time.pattern_override preferences would appear to be the wayt to do this. See https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/kb/customize-date-time-formats-thunderbird#w_create-date-and-time-format-override-preferences-using-thunderbirds-config-editor

See as my references https://kb.mozillazine.org/Date_display_format and https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/kb/customize-date-time-formats-thunderbird

You have Thunderbird installed on a computer but you haven't launched Thunderbird for 9 months. You have now started it after that long recess but now it seems hung up on retrieving messages. It appears that the folder indexing process is what's stuck. Correct? Is Thunderbird otherwise responsive?

The indexing process can, indeed, take a lot of time, but 12 hours is not normal. Although not ideal, at this point I think you have to kill the process. Folder indexes can become corrupt and could possibly lead to symptoms like this. However, if necessary, an index can be deleted and will rebuild itself as needed.

Don't try to upgrade Thunderbird until your installed version is working correctly.

I just looked at your topic for your Thunderbird for android. I do not use it, but I find the whole concept of "pretending" to send mail to be a concept I can not swallow. I would be far more agreeable if the mail was not getting delivered. There are many causes of send mail not arriving. I am not aware of a single instance of a send failure not reporting an error. But then do desktop because I really don't trust the phone operating system for much more than I have to and the Phone version is based on another code base to the desktop version, so I sort of question if it can be called the same product, about the only thing they share is the name. However I am unable to find any credible reports of silent failures to send mail. Did you really not get anything in your android notifications? Perhaps post the source headers from the sent folder as they will contain some diagnostic information.

As for this latest issue, I think we really need to be looking as the hosting provider. What is the domain part of the email? It should not be hard to work out who hosts the domain and from there we may well find you are using the Microsoft exchange product (proprietary protocols) and that would largely explain both your phone issues and this.

As for the indexing issue, you can kill Thunderbird while indexing is occurring, as a matter of fact in this instance I suggest you delete the index and force it to start again. See https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/kb/rebuilding-global-database

Do you have an antivirus on that apple product? They cause numerable issues with their processing and removal while diagnosing issues is a very good idea..
creator: MattAuSupport

content:
So, this is the same computer that was formerly "nonfunctional" but is now functional? Or is this a different computer with a fresh installation of Thunderbird on it?

I'm guessing you had it set up as a POP3 account using the option to '"Leave messages on server." This setting ensures that emails are not automatically deleted from the server after being downloaded. But I wonder if Thunderbird is now confused and doesn't know what was formerly downloaded and what wasn't. (This is a weakness of POP3 - it's fragile - and why I prefer IMAP.)

Yes, I think killing it is your only option at this point. But before you restart Thunderbird you should make a backup copy of your Profile, in case something goes wrong. In Thunderbird, go to the menu option:

Help -> Troubleshooting Information -> Open Folder

to open your Profile folder. Go up one level to the Profile's containing folder. Now close down Thunderbird and copy the Profile folder to somewhere safe. (It's good practice to make regular backups of that folder, especially in the case of POP3 accounts where the only copy of your old messages is likely to be on your local computer.)

I'm hoping that when you next start Thunderbird it will successfully download waiting messages and do its indexing chore and you'll be back to normal. In other words, the hangup was 'just one of them crazy computer things.'

Please let me know how it goes, and if not resolved we can take it from there.
